coredata.dc:title: Pressure and polymer selections for solid-state batteries investigated with high-throughput simulations
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xin
Luo, Changqi
Menga, Nicola
Zhang, Hao
Li, Yanxin
Zhu, Shun-Peng
coredata.dc:description: Polymer coatings and high mechanical pressure are promising solutions for improving interfacial contact in all-solid-state lithium metal batteries. However, design guidelines for polymer type, thickness, and stack pressure are still missing. In this study, we present a model for mechanics at the interface of polymer-coated solid-state electrolytes in contact with a lithium metal anode, considering lithium creep, polymer viscoelasticity, and pressure-driven electrochemistry. We cover various common polymer coatings, eventually highlighting the dependence of interfacial resistance on stack pressure and coating thickness. A machine learning algorithm with high-throughput calculations is used to optimize the combination of pressure and coating thicknesses. Numerical results are in good agreement with existing experimental evidence. A transition map is derived, which may serve as design guideline in predicting the values of current density, stack pressure, and polymeric thickness able to ensure a steady performance over time.

head.ce:title: Pressure and polymer selections for solid-state batteries investigated with high-throughput simulations

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymer coatings and high mechanical pressure are promising solutions for improving interfacial contact in all-solid-state lithium metal batteries. However, design guidelines for polymer type, thickness, and stack pressure are still missing. In this study, we present a model for mechanics at the interface of polymer-coated solid-state electrolytes in contact with a lithium metal anode, considering lithium creep, polymer viscoelasticity, and pressure-driven electrochemistry. We cover various common polymer coatings, eventually highlighting the dependence of interfacial resistance on stack pressure and coating thickness. A machine learning algorithm with high-throughput calculations is used to optimize the combination of pressure and coating thicknesses. Numerical results are in good agreement with existing experimental evidence. A transition map is derived, which may serve as design guideline in predicting the values of current density, stack pressure, and polymeric thickness able to ensure a steady performance over time.

Polymer-ceramic hybrid solid electrolytes can mitigate interface instability in lithium solid-state batteries. Here, Zhang, Luo, and Menga et al. create high-throughput simulations to select optimal pressures and polymer coatings for interface improvement.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data are available from the lead contact upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para:
All solid-state lithium metal batteries (ASSLMBs) represent a promising technology for next-generation energy storage systems due to their higher energy density, lower self-discharge rate, and better safety in use. For these reasons, ASSLMBs are expected to have large-scale applications in electrochemical energy storage systems and electric vehicles. 1
,
2
,
3 However, replacing a liquid electrolyte with solid-state electrolytes (SSEs) leads to several intrinsic challenges, including poor interfacial contact between lithium metal and SSEs, low ionic conductivity, high interfacial resistance, and inevitable brittle cracks along the grain boundaries in ceramic electrolytes. 4
,
5
,
6
,
7
,
8 Methods to solve those interfacial problems in ASSLMBs include mechanics-guided external pressure implementation, 9
,
10
,
11
,
12
,
13 electrochemistry-based interface modification, 14
,
15
,
16
,
17
,
18
,
19 material micro-structural optimization of solid electrolytes and electrodes, 8
,
20
,
21
,
22
,
23
,
24
,
25 and recent physics-induced techniques, such as an ultrasonic solid welding strategy, for interfacial improvements. 26

Among these interfacial improvement methods, combining solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs) and inorganic ceramic electrolytes (ICEs) to form solid composite electrolytes leads to acceptable ionic conductivity (owing to ICEs) and high mechanical ductility (owing to SPEs), thus resulting in favorable interfacial contact behavior with electrodes. Indeed, a better electrochemical performance of the all-solid-state batteries has been reported, compared with SPEs or ICEs. 27
,
28 Generally, composite electrolytes are fabricated by adding ceramics (nanoparticles, 29
,
30
,
31 nanowires, 32
,
33
,
34
,
35 nanosheets 36
,
37 ) into a polymer matrix, or bonding a polymer coating to a ceramic substrate. 14
,
15 The hybrid SSEs are achieved by searching for better polymer-ceramic mixing strategies guided by particle/fiber size distribution and coating thickness through trial and error, which may result in exhausting efforts. Therefore, the definition of a unified design principle would significantly benefit the polymer-ceramics mixing manufacture by defining the optimal mass fraction, particle/fiber size distribution, coating thickness, and polymer types.

The bulk microstructures and interfacial characteristics of SSEs strongly influence the dendrite growth, crack propagation, and determine the initial conditions of electrochemomechanical dynamics in lithium metal solid-state batteries. 8 Led by trial and error strategies, several experiments have been performed with different composite SSEs, 38
,
39
,
40
,
41
,
42 aiming at optimizing the composite with the best combination of mass fraction, particle/fiber size distribution, coating thickness, and polymer types. Influential studies on the evaluation of polymer properties on electrodeposition have been carried out by Bao’s and Cui’s group, 16 as well as those on influences of SSE thickness on ionic conductance by Huang’s and Li’s group. 43 From the perspective of simulations, existing investigations include the study of SSE material behaviors by means of energy balance approaches, 44 SSE coating thickness influence on interface chemical stability by a theoretical model, 45 void evolution at lithium-SSE interface by phase field method, 46 and the effect of Young’s modulus on Li ion transport by finite element method. 47 Furthermore, contact mechanics theories 48
,
49
,
50
,
51
,
52 and calculations 53
,
54
,
55 have shown that the contact pressure also plays a fundamental role in determining the effective contact area at the interface, which, in turn, also affects the contact resistance of the lithium-SSE interface. This has also been shown in the field of interfaces involving SSEs by experiments 11
,
12
,
56
,
57 and tailored calculations. 9
,
58
,
59
,
60
,
61
,
62
,
63

Multiple, synergistic, and multifield (physical, mechanical, electrochemical) factors determine the battery performance, as highlighted in Sun and co-workers 64
,
65 and Wu and Bo, 66 making it hard to achieve both a complete experimental characterization of these electrolyte materials and a numerical simulation of the interface, due to increasing computational cost. This latter issue has been partially mitigated by relying on machine learning algorithms, as demonstrated in the field of material engineering for robust design of composite electrolytes. 67
,
68
,
69
,
70 A physics-guided artificial intelligence technology has now become an emerging tool in the field of lithium metal battery development, 71
,
72 because machine learning can both make accurate decisions based on big data 73 (obtained by experiments or physical models) and predict mechanical and electrochemical performance of electrolyte materials, thereby significantly shortening the composite SSEs development cycle.

Various computational methods provide original training data for machine learning of composite ASSE development, including first-principle simulations, 44
,
74 the phase field method, 58
,
75
,
76 and the finite element method. 47
,
77
,
78 Those methods focus on the influences of local mechanical stresses at the micron-scale, or are concentrated primarily on events that occur at a single timescale. In reality, however, Li metal has a strong tendency to creep, and polymer viscoelasticity is known to play an important role in battery degradation. 79
,
80
,
81 To better capture the overall properties of electrode-electrolyte interfaces, we propose a multi-scale contact mechanics method to calculate lithium-ceramics, 9 sodium-ceramics, 60 and ceramics-cathode 82 interfacial behaviors.

Now, we further develop a computational model for interface contact between lithium and a polymer-ceramics composite electrolyte. Several studies have shown that, when dealing with contacts involving thin layers, the effect of the layer’s finite thickness on the overall contact behavior cannot be neglected, both for elastic 83
,
84
,
85
,
86 and viscoelastic 87
,
88
,
89
,
90 contacts. Therefore, to enhance the accuracy of electrode-electrolyte interface behavior, here we specifically model the finite thickness of the viscoelastic polymer coating. Indeed, by exploiting the Green’s function approach and a continuous convolution-fast Fourier transform (CC-FT) algorithm, our 3D rough contact mechanics simulations are specifically designed to take into account the transient interface behaviors due to the lithium creep, lithium elastoplasticity, polymer viscoelasticity, and pressure-driven electrochemistry (dendrite growth). The contact simulation results are then used as a training set for a machine learning algorithm, which allows for further contact mechanics calculations with high-throughput screening. Eventually, by relying on these machine learning calculations, our study highlights the relationship between coating thickness and interfacial resistance as a function of the stack pressures, for PDMS (polydimethylsiloxane), PP (polypropylene), PU (polyurethane), PVDF (polyvinylidene fluoride), PVDF-HFP (polyvinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene), PVCA (polyvinylene carbonate), and PE (polyethylene) coatings on oxide SSEs. We also calculate a favorable pressure limit for long-time and stable electrochemical cycles in the presence of surface morphological alterations (plating), which are in agreement with existing experimental observations and previous theoretical findings.

In this work, we focused on the anode-side interface of the polymer-ceramic-layered SSEs in contact with the Li metal under a stack pressure,
P
. Figure 1 A illustrates the 3D contact problem relating to the nature of a cell structure. A polymer of thickness
h
P o l y
is coated on the ceramics, forming a polymer-ceramic-layered SSE, to improve the anode-SSE interfacial contact. Since real surfaces always present a certain degree of roughness, the real contact area at the lithium-polymer interface differs from the nominal contact area. 91
,
92 To reduce the computational cost, as shown in Figures 1B and 1C, calculations focus on a square portion of the interface of size
L c
×
L c

L c
= 400

.

Since increasing the real contact area leads to lower interfacial electrical resistance, the contact fraction, defined by
F c
=
c o n t a c t
a r e a

, is used to characterize the lithium-polymer interfacial contact performance, where the nominal area is defined as
L c
×
L c
, while the real contact area can be determined by the contact mechanics solver (see experimental procedures). Generally, the lithium metal surface is assumed to be smooth, while the polymer surface is rough (roughness data are provided in the experimental procedures).

Existing experimental literature focuses on different polymers as coating materials for the oxide or sulfide SSEs, such as the poly(oxyethylene) (PEO)-coated Li1.5Al0.5Ge1.5(PO4)3 (LAGP), 93
,
94 Li1.3Al0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3, 95 or Ta-doped Li7La3Zr2O12, 96 the PP-coated LAGP, 97 the PVCA-coated LAGP, 98 and the PEO coated Li10GeP2S12 (LGPS). 99 In our simulations, we always target to polymers softer than the oxides (i.e., <<100 GPa), as shown in Tables 1 and S1.

Figure 2 A shows the contact fraction
F c
at the lithium-polymer interface as a function of the stack pressure value. Different oxide SSE coating polymers are investigated, whereas the coating thickness is kept constant at a value of 30
μ m
. The behavior of bare oxide SSEs without polymer coatings is also shown for comparison. In agreement with Menga et al., 83
,
88
,
100 the coatings of PDMS, PP, and PU entails much higher contact fractions, as these polymers are relatively soft (i.e., low elastic modulus). Moreover, for these coatings, we found that the pressure threshold for full contact is about 10 kPa, with the PDMS coating able to ensure a contact fraction as high as 90% at a stack pressure of 1 kPa. Differently, coatings of PEO, PVDF, PVDF-HFP, PVCA, or PE lead to stiffer contact interfaces, so that contact fraction values of about 80% are achieved for stack pressure in the range of 100–500 kPa. For comparison, we observe that, under 1 MPa stack pressure, the contact fraction for bare oxide SSE results in lower than 20%, so that existing studies prescribe a minimum stack pressure of about 20 MPa to sustain the contact in this case. 9

In contact mechanics, the contact stiffness is defined by the ratio of mechanical load and indentation depth, i.e.,
K =
d P
d δ
. In the case of non-conformal contacts between a rigid indenter (metal) and a softer body (SSE), under a constant compressive load, lower contact stiffness means deeper indentation, which also entails higher contact fraction. The contact stiffness depends on the contacting materials, geometry, and so on. Larger modulus indicates larger contact stiffness. For example, in the Hertzian contact, the contact stiffness is,

Here, we have two contacting bodies: the lithium metal and the polymer-ceramics system, as shown in Figure 1A. For the polymer-ceramics system, since the polymer is softer than the ceramics, increasing the polymer thickness means decreasing the effective modulus of the polymer-ceramics system, thus reducing the contact stiffness. On the other hand, if the coating is stiffer than the substrate, increasing the coating thickness will get the opposite trend. Regarding the effect of coating thickness under given normal pressure, as shown in Figure 2B, we observe that, dealing with coatings softer than the base oxide SSE, the thicker the coating the lower the contact stiffness is; therefore, thicker coatings lead to higher contact fraction values, in agreement with Menga et al. 83
,
88
,
100

The final purpose of coating the SE surface with soft polymers is to reduce the overall Li-SSE electric resistance by increasing the contact conductivity at the contact interface. This is achieved by introducing soft coatings able to significantly increase the contact fraction between the coating polymer and the hard Li metal. However, the polymeric coating presents a much lower ionic conductivity than the original oxide SSE, which might eventually entail the opposite effect, i.e., a reduction of the overall electrical conductivity of the Li-polymer-SSE system. To avoid such a scenario, oxide additives have been dispersed into the polymer matrix to form composite SSE coatings. Under these conditions, the homogenized elastic modulus,
E

Different types of oxide particles are dispersed into the PEO, PVDF, PVCA, and PDMS polymers to form composite SSE coatings. The homogenized elastic modulus and effective Poisson’s ratio of the composite SSEs for various weight ratios are calculated by Equation 1, and listed in Table 2 , for room temperature. Also, the ionic conductivities of the composite SSEs, with different weight ratios, have been measured by referenced experiments (at different temperatures), and are listed in Table 2. Notably, since the data sources are different, we assume that the mechanical properties and the ionic conductivity are measured after a strict drying process (i.e., neglecting any effect of immobilized solvents).

The mechanical properties reported in Table 2 indicate that increasing the weight ration of ceramic particles leads to stiffer composite polymers and, in turn, to lower values of the contact fraction. Indeed, Figure 3 A shows the contact fraction values for different combinations of ceramic particles and polymer matrix. We notice that the specific contact fraction value is correlated with initial interfacial resistance, which is a key factor for the subsequent electrochemical evolution of the system. Moreover, Figure 3A only focuses on spherical particles, whereas ceramics fibers and sheets additives may result in different contact fractions. 32
,

As discussed so far, a thicker polymer coating leads to a better contact with the lithium anode (i.e., higher value of
F c
and lower interfacial resistance, see Figure 2B); however, it also entails a higher electrolyte bulk resistance. On the contrary, a thinner polymer leads to a higher contact stiffness (hence a higher interfacial resistance) but a smaller bulk resistance. Therefore, the overall resistance between the lithium and SSEs is given by

We simulated the effect of the coating thickness on the lithium and SSE total resistance,
R
t o t
, for different types of coating polymers deposited onto an LLZO SSE under the action of a stack pressure of 1000 kPa (Figure 3B). PEO-LLTO, PEO-SiO2, PVDF-LLTO, PEO-TiO2, and PEO-LGPS are polymer-based composite materials with 10 wt % oxide particles or sulfide particles. The ionic conductivities of those polymer-based composite materials are listed in Table 2. Notably, although room temperature data are assumed for coating mechanical properties, the available ionic conductivity data refer to higher temperature measurements. Bare PEO denotes a pure polymer coating without particle additives. More in detail, according to Renna et al., 110 for the Li-PEO interface we set
A S R = 13.05
k Ω

2
. We also simulated the bare LLZO case (no coating); in this case, according to Han et al., 17 we set
A S R = 109
Ω

. Notably, in the latter case
R

= 0
so that, according to Equation 4, the only source of resistance is the interfacial contact (i.e.,
R
t o t
=
R int
). By exploiting the contact mechanics model described in the experimental procedures, we performed several calculations allowing to define a range of coating thicknesses able to minimize the overall Li-SSE electrical resistance compared with the bare Li-LLZO interface. This is shown in the yellow shaded region in Figure 3B, where the overall resistance of the Li-SEE interface is shown as a function of the polymeric coating thickness. As expected, the resistance is a non-monotonic function of the coating thickness, as for both very thin and very thick coating the conductivity of the interface is reduced. In the former case, this is mostly due to the large contact stiffness of thin coatings, which entails small contact area. On the contrary, in the latter scenario (thick coating), the poor polymer ionic conductivity leads to increased bulk resistance. Interestingly, depending on the specific coating polymer, the optimal coating thickness where the overall electric resistance is minimized varies.

To calculate the combined effect of stack pressure, coating thicknesses, and coating material on the total resistance of the Li-SSE interface, several simulations are required (see Figures S1 and S2). For the sake of a significant reduction of the computation time, we developed a high-throughput calculation-based machine learning approach (Figure 3C; details see experimental procedures). Specifically, support vector regression (SVR) has been implemented as regression model to predict the internal resistance as a function of coating material properties (elastic modulus, Poisson’s ratio), stack pressure, and polymer thickness. As the training set for the machine learning algorithm, we used the data obtained from a large amount of contact mechanics computations (e.g., Figures 3A, 3B, S1, and S2).

Eight polymer types and six stack pressure values have been chosen (868 contact mechanics simulation) as the dataset. The results associated with five stack pressure values (757 sets of results) have been used as training data, whereas the remaining data (111 sets of results) as the validation cases. After K-fold cross-validation with Bayes optimization (see Figure 3C) by 200 iterations, 121
,
122 we got a set of hyperparameters:
X p =
[
826.0242 , 0.7369 , 0.0166
]
. The machine learning results of training data and test data are shown in Figure 3D. The Pearson correlation coefficients
R
of training data and test data are 0.99908 and 0.99832, respectively, and the root mean-square errors (RMSEs) of them are 0.045922 and 0.063351, respectively, indicating good results on the training data and a good generalization ability for the test data (for details see Figures S3–S5).

Then we use the SVR model 123
,
124 to find appropriate ranges for combinations of stack pressure, coating thickness, and material types (e.g., yellow regions in Figure 3B), approaching lower and upper limits for pressure and thickness. Figures 3E and 3F show the thickness-pressure transition maps for PEO coatings, with stack pressure values in the range of 0–4,000 kPa and above 10,000 kPa, respectively. The transition maps for other polymer coatings are shown in Figure S6. The transition maps show that, for values of the coating thickness and stack pressure laying within the blue regions, the total resistance of coated oxide SSE is lower than that of the bare oxide, thus indicating that the coating is beneficial for the interface performance. Specifically, in the case of a PEO coating, under a stack pressure value of 144 kPa, the optimal coating thickness is about
26
μ m
, in very good agreement with experimental results indicating a thickness ranges from 16 to 26
μ
m. 110 Wu et al. 43 also indicated that the polymer thickness should be controlled below 30
μ
m. 111
,
112
,
113 In the limiting case of vanishing thickness (i.e., for bare oxide SSE), a stack pressure value in the range of 30–40 MPa is required to achieve unimprovable interfacial performance (see Figures 3F and S7), in agreement with the experiment findings 114 and model prediction. 9

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic competition between mechanics and electrochemistry
ce:section.ce:para: During the charging or discharging process, the Li-SSE contact interface undergoes electrochemical reactions and chemomechanical deformations (plating), which lead to time-dependent interface behavior. In the contact mechanics framework, the mechanical deformations include the elastoplastic deformation of lithium metal, the time-dependent creep of lithium metal, and the elastic or viscoelastic deformation of polymers. In general, the mechanical deformations lead to increased contact area and to reduced total resistance; on the contrary, the chemomechanical plating tends to increase the lithium surface heights (i.e., lower contact area and higher total resistance). The result depends on the balance between the competing action of mechanics and electrochemistry.

Figure 4 A plots the interfacial potential change
V
in a 2.5-h plating process, under an electrode current density of
I = 0.2
m A /
c m
2
, for different stack pressure values. Notably, since
V = I ·
R int
, recalling Equation 3, the lower the value of the interfacial potential, the higher the contact fraction is. We observe that the interfacial potential gradually decreases with time due to the pressure-driven lithium metal creep.

The competition between mechanics and electrochemistry can be identified by the parameter
K
m , e
=
m e c h a n i c s
e l e c t r o c h e m i s t y
=
l i t h i u m
c r e e p + l i t h i u m

d e f o r m a t i o n
p l a t i n g

We simulated combined effects of different stack pressure and applied current values on the parameter
K
m , e
(see Figures S9–S20). Focusing on Figure 4D (for a current density value of 0.2 mA/cm2), we observe that
K
m , e
> 1
(blue region) indicates that the interfacial behavior mostly depends on mechanical phenomena, so that the interface performance is enhanced over time. On the contrary,
K
m , e
< 1
(yellow region) indicates that the interface performance is mainly governed by plating, and Li dendrites and cracks may occur and the interface performance decrease with time. In conclusion, as summarized in Figure 4F, for any given value of current density, a minimum value of stack pressure exists above which the plating effect does not significantly reduce the contact fraction and, in turn, the total resistance of the interface.

To optimize the combinations of pressure and current, several simulations have been performed (see Figures S9–S20). A transition map is reported in Figure 4E to evaluate the combined effects of current and pressure on the contact interface performance. Particularly, for the current density value of 0.1 mA/cm2, a pressure of at least 3 kPa is required to maintain a good contact fraction, while a pressure value of at least 1 MPa is needed to improve the contact interface under current density of 2.0 mA/cm2. Without polymer coatings, the required pressure for bare ceramic SSE is larger than 20 MPa. 9

Figure 4E summarizes the threshold pressure value required to ensure a stable interface behavior as a function of the applied current density, while the limiting pressures as a function of the areal capacity is also concluded in Figure S21. These data can be used as practical guidelines to design Li-SSE interfaces in terms of mechanical pressure, current density, and plating capacity.

In summary, we have developed a contact mechanics model for the Li-metal and SSE interface based on the Green’s function approach and the CC-FT solving algorithm. The aim of this study is to set up guiding principles to choose the optimal value of stack pressure, coating material, and coating thickness to minimize the overall performance of the electrode interface in all-solid-state lithium metal batteries. The model considers the lithium creep, lithium elastoplasticity, polymer viscoelasticity, pressure-driven electrochemistry, and contact surface roughness to predict the interfacial contact dynamics as a function of the applied stack pressure, polymer type, polymer thickness, and current density. Specifically, the study focuses on PDMS, PP, PU, PEO, PVDF, PVCA, and PE polymer coatings. A machine learning algorithm with high-throughput calculation was adopted to speed up the contact calculations necessary for the performance optimization. Results show that the stack pressure greatly affects the interfacial contact behavior, as increasing the stack pressure leads to higher values of contact fraction. Similarly, due to the contact stiffness of polymers, the fraction in contact also increases with the increase in the polymer thickness.

Ceramic-lithium metal electrolytes under polymer coatings were considered. Due to the internal resistance including interfacial and bulk resistances, internal resistance as a whole shows a trend of first decreasing (contact dominated) and then increasing (bulk resistance dominated). Furthermore, a contact-model-based machine learning algorithm with high-throughput calculation was performed, and its efficiency was greatly improved. Under the framework of SVR, the relationship between stack pressure and polymer thickness in cell fabrication is presented for cell performance improvement. Particularly, with an applied pressure as small as 144 kPa, a required coating of about
26
μ m
thickness is critical to maintain a lower resistance, identical to existing experimental thickness ranges from 16 to 26 μm. 43
,
110
,
111
,
112
,
113 Moreover, the contact behavior at the interface of lithium metal batteries varies during charging and discharging. Therefore, plating calculations have been performed to define a transition map able to account for the combined effects of current and pressure on the contact interface performance over time. The results show that larger current densities require larger stack pressures to keep high contact fraction values and, hence, to ensure good interfacial performance over time. Particularly, a pressure of at least 3 kPa is required for a better contact for a current of 0.1 mA/cm2, while at least 1 MPa pressure is needed to improve the interface under a current of 2.0 mA/cm2. The guiding principles disclosed here may prove beneficial for the development of future solid-state batteries.
Further information and requests for resources should be directed to and will be fulfilled by the lead contact, Xin Zhang (zhangxin@uestc.edu.cn).
This study did not generate new, unique reagents.

The contact mechanics calculation has been performed on the roughness resulting from measurements performed on a well-polished LLZO surface using a Bruker Dektak XT Stylus Profilometer, over an area of
400 × 400
μ m
. We take this surface to be representative of a referenced solid-electrolyte rough surface. Specifically, the measured surface roughness presented a root mean-square height
h
r m s
= 13
n m
, maximum asperity height
h max
= 43
n m
, and a root mean-square gradient
g ¯
≈ 0.0011
. Notably, roughness data are in agreement with previous measurements by Sakamoto and co-workers 9
,
11 on similar surfaces.

The contact solver can be found in the work of Wang and co-workers 9
,
91 and the viscoelastic contact of coatings are based on the simulation in Wang and co-workers. 92 Building on several well-known contact mechanics studies, 83
,

100 the contact model relies on a Green’s function approach, with closing conditions on the stress distribution at the edge of the contact area. The solution scheme is based on the CC-FT algorithm. 59
,
91
,
125 The model accurately predicts the displacement and stress field at the interface between the Li metal and the SSE, also taking into account the elastoplasticity and viscoelastic creep of the two solids.

The simulation domain has been discretized into a
400 × 400
subset mesh grid. An incremental step of 50 ms is selected for creep and viscoelastic computation. For every 10 s, the plating-induced surface geometry is updated based on the mechanical-electrochemistry calculations (see Equations 9 and 10). For a 2.5-h plating process, we conducted 180,001 contact simulations, 18,000 creep computations, and 900 mechanical-electrochemistry calculations.

Machine learning is a multi-domain interdisciplinary method. We established a machine learning model to achieve various purposes, such as regression, classification, and clustering of data. As a supervised learning algorithm, SVR 124 is widely used due to its sparsity and robustness. Generally, machine learning algorithms can obtain lower errors for training data, but for data other than training data, the prediction accuracy depends on the specific machine learning method. In the case of the SVR model, the generalization ability is mainly determined by three hyperparameters, namely, penalty coefficient
C
, kernel parameter
σ
, and loss distance metric
ε
. Therefore, aiming at defining the optimal set of hyperparameters, we performed a Bayes optimization, with objective function

i
so that the surrogate function takes the maximum value; (3) evaluate the objective function results

X p
i
; (4) update the surrogate function with the new data
X p
i
to obtain a successive distribution (i.e., the initial distribution at the next step); (5) Repeat (1)–(4) until the stopping criterion is matched.

Moreover, the K-fold cross-validation is introduced to improve the generalization error of the SVR model. In this work, we assume that there are materials in p, and then select materials in k (k<p) as initial data, k–1 materials as training data, and 1 material as validation data.
